front: Figure: the 16th President of the United States, who led the nation through the Civil War and was assassinated by John Wilkes Booth
work: General / Giveaway Identifiers

front: Figure: popularly nicknamed "Honest Abe" and "the Great Emancipator"
work: General / Giveaway Identifiers

front: Figure: earned the nickname "Honest Abe" for returning shortchanged cash to customers while working at a general store in Illinois
work: Early Life and Political Rise

front: Figure: popularly depicted carrying rail fences and an axe, an image popularized by John Locke Scripps
work: Early Life and Political Rise

front: Figure: Alexander McClure and Horatio Alger collected myths about him
work: Early Life and Political Rise

front: Figure: his only military service came as a volunteer militia captain during the Black Hawk War
work: Early Life and Political Rise

front: Figure: as a lawyer, intentionally spent months each year away from his wife while riding a judicial circuit, according to Dale Carnegie's biography
work: Early Life and Political Rise

front: Figure: patented an invention to buoy grounded vessels over shoals, inspired by the grounding of the steamboat Canada
work: Early Life and Political Rise

front: Event: seven 1858 debates against incumbent Democratic Senator Stephen Douglas for an Illinois Senate seat, which Lincoln ultimately lost
work: The Lincoln-Douglas Debates and the Road to the Presidency

front: Event: began in Ottawa and ended in Alton, Illinois; reporters used Pitman shorthand to cover these events
work: The Lincoln-Douglas Debates and the Road to the Presidency

front: Event: at the second of these debates, held in Freeport, Stephen Douglas presented his "Freeport Doctrine"
work: The Lincoln-Douglas Debates and the Road to the Presidency

front: Figure: declared "A house divided against itself cannot stand" during his losing 1858 Senate campaign against Stephen Douglas
work: The Lincoln-Douglas Debates and the Road to the Presidency

front: Event: in this 1860 speech at Cooper Union in New York, Lincoln argued the Constitution allowed the federal government to restrict the expansion of slavery, raising his national profile
work: The Lincoln-Douglas Debates and the Road to the Presidency

front: Figure: delivered his Lyceum Address after a mob lynching in St. Louis, arguing that rebuilding the "pillar of liberty" required "the solid quarry of sober reason"
work: The Lincoln-Douglas Debates and the Road to the Presidency

front: Figure: won the presidency in the Election of 1860, defeating Stephen Douglas and John Bell
work: The Lincoln-Douglas Debates and the Road to the Presidency

front: Event: suspended in 1861 to allow the arbitrary arrest of Southern sympathizers, taken eight days after Baltimore's Pratt Street Riots and used to arrest that city's mayor and city council
work: Wartime Presidency: Policy and the Union Cause

front: Event: Roger Taney ruled this action unconstitutional in Ex parte Merryman, though Lincoln defended it in a letter to Matthew Birchard
work: Wartime Presidency: Policy and the Union Cause

front: Event: an 1863 statute codified this action, allowing Ambrose Burnside to arrest Ohio Copperhead Clement Vallandigham
work: Wartime Presidency: Policy and the Union Cause

front: Event: Francis Scott Key's grandson Frank Key Howard was imprisoned in Fort McHenry for criticizing this action
work: Wartime Presidency: Policy and the Union Cause

front: Work: examined this action in his Pulitzer-winning history The Fate of Liberty, which called it "well-intentioned"
work: Wartime Presidency: Policy and the Union Cause

front: Figure: commuted the death sentences of 264 Dakota after the 1862 Dakota War, but approved the hanging of the remaining 38, the largest mass execution in American history
work: Wartime Presidency: Policy and the Union Cause

front: Figure: signed the first Homestead Act, encouraging settlement of the West
work: Wartime Presidency: Policy and the Union Cause

front: Figure: personally served as direct commander of all Union armies for four months between the terms of generals-in-chief McClellan and Halleck
work: Wartime Presidency: Policy and the Union Cause

front: Event: Lincoln gave a brief speech, later known by this name, following a two-hour oration by Edward Everett
work: Wartime Presidency: Policy and the Union Cause

front: Event: his first inaugural address (1861) pledged not to fill offices with "obnoxious strangers" and invoked the "mystic chords of memory" and "the better angels of our nature"
work: Wartime Presidency: Policy and the Union Cause

front: Event: his second inaugural address (1865) rejected triumphalism and closed "with malice toward none, with charity for all"
work: Wartime Presidency: Policy and the Union Cause

front: Figure: defended against Lerone Bennett's 1968 essay asking "Was Lincoln a White Supremacist?" by historians including Eric Foner, whose book The Fiery Trial chronicles his acceptance of abolitionism and issuance of the Emancipation Proclamation
work: Wartime Presidency: Policy and the Union Cause

front: Work: this Doris Kearns Goodwin book examines Lincoln's often-fractious relationship with a cabinet full of men who had wanted his job
back: Team of Rivals: The Political Genius of Abraham Lincoln (Doris Kearns Goodwin)
work: Wartime Presidency: Policy and the Union Cause

front: Figure: his private secretaries John Hay and John George Nicolay wrote an early ten-volume critical biography of him
work: Wartime Presidency: Policy and the Union Cause

front: Event: shot by John Wilkes Booth during a performance of Our American Cousin at Ford's Theatre, moments after a character said "you sockdologizing old man-trap"
work: Assassination and the Body

front: Event: this thing's funeral train retraced the route of his original inauguration from Washington, D.C. to Springfield, Illinois, carrying the remains of his son Willie alongside his own
work: Assassination and the Body

front: Event: in 1876, counterfeiter "Big Jim" Kennally plotted to steal this thing from Oak Ridge Cemetery and hold it for ransom, planning to bury it in the Indiana Dunes, but was foiled by Secret Service informants
work: Assassination and the Body

front: Event: John Carroll Power formed a "Guard of Honor" to protect this thing
work: Assassination and the Body

front: Event: in 1901, Fleetwood Lindley became the last living person to see this thing when it was exhumed, finding that its eyebrows had completely vanished
work: Assassination and the Body

front: Figure: turned down an earlier marriage proposal from Stephen Douglas before marrying Abraham Lincoln
work: Mary Todd Lincoln and the Todd Family

front: Figure: three of her brothers, Sam, David, and Alexander Todd, served as Confederate officers and died at Shiloh, Vicksburg, and Baton Rouge respectively
work: Mary Todd Lincoln and the Todd Family

front: Figure: her half-sister Emilie Todd Helm, widow of a Confederate general, was allowed to remain in Washington, D.C. despite refusing to swear a loyalty oath
work: Mary Todd Lincoln and the Todd Family

front: Event: Mary Todd Lincoln sold off her wardrobe to settle scrutinized jewelry debts in this controversy
work: Mary Todd Lincoln and the Todd Family

front: Figure: the formerly enslaved seamstress Elizabeth Keckley became her confidante and later wrote a memoir defending her
work: Mary Todd Lincoln and the Todd Family

front: Figure: held numerous seances in the White House, with the help of mediums, to try to contact her deceased sons Eddie and Willie
work: Mary Todd Lincoln and the Todd Family

front: Figure: scandalized Washington by wearing lilac instead of black mourning at a funeral, asking why she should "muffle myself in mourning for every soldier killed"
work: Mary Todd Lincoln and the Todd Family

front: Figure: her son Robert Todd Lincoln had her committed to a mental institution after a series of shopping binges, leading her to attempt suicide by overdosing on laudanum
work: Mary Todd Lincoln and the Todd Family

front: Figure: received a retrial that overturned her confinement after smuggling a letter to activist Myra Bradwell
work: Mary Todd Lincoln and the Todd Family

front: Event: this Henry Bacon-designed memorial contains a Daniel Chester French sculpture of Lincoln seated with his hands on his knees, inside a building with 36 Doric columns overlooking a reflecting pool
work: Memorials and Monuments

front: Event: Marian Anderson gave a landmark 1939 Easter Sunday concert here, organized by Secretary of the Interior Harold Ickes, after the Daughters of the American Revolution barred her from Constitution Hall
work: Memorials and Monuments

front: Event: Martin Luther King Jr. delivered the "I Have a Dream" speech on its steps during the March on Washington
work: Memorials and Monuments

front: Institution: a sculpture of Lincoln by this artist, the first government commission awarded to a female artist, stands in the Capitol rotunda opposite one of Grant
work: Memorials and Monuments

front: Event: depicted on the obverse of these coins, designed in part by Victor D. Brenner
work: Memorials and Monuments

front: Event: this transcontinental road, roughly paralleled today by US Route 30, is dotted with statues of Lincoln
work: Memorials and Monuments

front: Event: on this road, a young Dwight Eisenhower once pranked a 1919 military convoy into fearing a Native American attack
work: Memorials and Monuments

front: Institution: this American volunteer unit of the International Brigades in the Spanish Civil War, led by Robert Hale Merriman, was named for Lincoln
work: Memorials and Monuments

front: Institution: this unit's members included Oliver Law, the first African American to lead white soldiers in battle
work: Memorials and Monuments

front: Work: elegizes Lincoln as "fallen cold and dead," part of the "Memories of Abraham Lincoln" cluster in Leaves of Grass
work: Legacy in Literature and Popular Culture

front: Work: describes a "western orb sailing the heaven" and "the great star early droop'd in the western sky," featuring the song of a hermit thrush, after Lincoln's assassination
back: "When Lilacs Last in the Dooryard Bloom'd" (Walt Whitman)
work: Legacy in Literature and Popular Culture

front: Work: written just days after Lincoln's assassination, this Whitman poem tells fellow soldiers to "drape our war-worn weapons"
work: Legacy in Literature and Popular Culture

front: Work: depicts Lincoln's ghost pacing sleeplessly near the old courthouse at midnight, unable to rest until "white peace" is achieved because "kings must murder still"
work: Legacy in Literature and Popular Culture

front: Work: this 2017 Booker Prize-winning novel depicts Lincoln visiting his son Willie's grave, while Willie is trapped among ghosts like Hans Vollman and Roger Bevins III in the Tibetan Buddhist "bardo"
work: Legacy in Literature and Popular Culture

front: Work: partly inspired by Edgar Lee Masters's ghost-narrated Spoon River Anthology, which includes Lincoln's first love, Ann Rutledge
work: Legacy in Literature and Popular Culture

front: Work: this Pulitzer-winning play, largely based on Carl Sandburg's biography, depicts Lincoln's youthful romance with Ann Rutledge
work: Legacy in Literature and Popular Culture

front: Work: centers on two brothers named Lincoln and Booth; Lincoln works at an arcade doing whiteface impersonations of the president and letting patrons "shoot" him
work: Legacy in Literature and Popular Culture

front: Work: this 2024 comedic play depicts Mary Todd Lincoln as a "well-known niche cabaret legend" who hides her husband's sexuality
work: Legacy in Literature and Popular Culture

front: Work: this Seth Grahame-Smith novel reimagines Lincoln's fight against slavery as a quest to end it by cutting off plantation owners' food supply while hunting vampires
work: Legacy in Literature and Popular Culture

front: Figure: photographer William Mumler produced a famous fraudulent "spirit photograph" of his ghost standing behind Mary Todd, later exposed with help from P.T. Barnum
work: Legacy in Literature and Popular Culture

front: Figure: Daniel Day-Lewis won an Academy Award for playing him in Steven Spielberg's 2012 film
work: Legacy in Literature and Popular Culture
